ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general clam food product class; no specific geoduck clam sashimi term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers raw clams and cockles as the nearest defensible category; no geoduck-specific code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for geoduck clam sashimi as a food product; omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 restaurant portion (approx. 150 g edible geoduck clam)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of geoduck clam sashimi is approximately 150 g of thinly sliced raw geoduck clam meat, plated with soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Geoduck, raw (Panopea generosa) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15158
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw geoduck clam (Panopea generosa) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent the raw edible portion per 100 g, consistent with sashimi preparation where no cooking is applied. Condiments (soy sauce, wasabi, ginger) are served alongside and not included in the per-100 g values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for geoduck clam sashimi. Raw shellfish and bivalves are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods (2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other raw shellfish (e.g., oysters, mussels, clams) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables, GI is estimated at approximately 5 (very low), consistent with the negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. GL per 150 g serving = (5 × 2.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.19, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (205 mg per 100 g); condiment sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Geoduck clam sashimi contains approximately 205 mg sodium per 100 g from the clam itself; individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid the accompanying soy sauce, which can add 400–900 mg sodium per tablespoon, and keep total portion size moderate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic index (~5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Geoduck clam sashimi is an excellent protein-rich, very low-carbohydrate option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible glycemic index and glycemic load pose minimal postprandial glucose impact, and the high protein content supports satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g), beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, high selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Geoduck clam sashimi is low in saturated fat and provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and selenium, which are associated with cardioprotective effects; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake from condiments served alongside.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Geoduck clam sashimi (象拔蚌刺身, Xiàng bá bàng cì shēn) is a premium raw seafood dish featuring thinly sliced geoduck clam (Panopea generosa), a large burrowing bivalve mollusc prized for its sweet, briny flavour and firm yet tender, slightly crunchy texture. The geoduck is one of the largest burrowing clams in the world, native to the Pacific Northwest coast of North America but widely imported into East and Southeast Asia for high-end seafood dining. In the Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char and seafood restaurant tradition, geoduck clam is typically served raw as sashimi, sliced paper-thin and presented chilled on ice, accompanied by Japanese-style condiments such as soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger, reflecting the cross-cultural culinary influences common in Singapore and Malaysia. The flesh has a clean oceanic sweetness with a mild brininess and a distinctive crisp-chewy bite that distinguishes it from softer bivalves such as oysters. Nutritionally, geoduck clam sashimi is an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g), very low in fat (0.9 g per 100 g) and carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g), and exceptionally rich in vitamin B12 (approximately 49 mcg per 100 g, far exceeding the adult RDA of 2.4 mcg), selenium, iron, and potassium. Its very low glycemic index (estimated ~5) makes it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids support cardiovascular health. Sodium content from the clam itself is moderate (~205 mg per 100 g), but diners should be mindful of additional sodium from soy sauce condiments. This dish is gluten-free and dairy-free in its base form. It is a luxury item in Zi Char and Chinese seafood restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia, often priced at a premium due to the cost of importing live geoduck clams.
common_user_queries: How many calories in geoduck clam sashimi?
Can diabetics eat geoduck clam sashimi?
What does geoduck clam taste like?
Is 象拔蚌 safe to eat raw?
Is geoduck clam high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw geoduck clam (Panopea generosa), which is an exact species match for the primary ingredient. The dish is served raw with no cooking transformation, so raw values are directly applicable. GI is estimated by analogy with other raw shellfish from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for geoduck clam exists in the published literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high due to the GI estimation and the absence of a Singapore/Malaysia-specific nutrient entry for this dish.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Gooduck Clam Sashiml'' contains a typographical error; the canonical name is 'Geoduck Clam Sashimi' (Panopea generosa). Corrected in this record. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw geoduck clam. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw geoduck clam. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero — raw bivalve shellfish contain no industrially produced trans fats; any trace geometric isomers are below detection threshold per USDA data. 5. sugar_g is 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw geoduck clam — shellfish contain negligible free sugars and no dietary fibre. 6. GI is estimated at approximately 5 by analogy with other raw shellfish (oysters, mussels, clams) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for geoduck clam exists in the published literature. 7. The cuisine tag 'Crab' in the input is a field survey platform tag and does not reflect the actual ingredient (geoduck clam); disregarded. 8. Halal status is marked false as geoduck clam (a bivalve mollusc) is not considered halal under the majority Shafi'i school ruling followed in Singapore and Malaysia, though opinions differ across madhabs. 9. The dish is served at Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay, Singapore, reflecting the Zi Char / Chinese seafood restaurant tradition. 10. Multilingual names for Malaysian cuisine context include Chinese (primary), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required by Veritas G6 for Singaporean/Malaysian dishes. Japanese name (ミルガイの刺身) included as the preparation style is Japanese sashimi.
